results.rationale: God produces dismay in rebels (Psa 2:5, 90:7) and commands against it in his people (Deu 1:21, 31:8), while his hidden face causes dismay (Psa 30:7; Job 23:16).
Fear belongs to the creaturely dimension; 2Ki 17:34-41 shows that true fear-of-God is an orientation defining covenant faithfulness, not a divine attribute.
Dismay registers genuine catastrophe (Job 4:5) and can be resisted when God has spoken (Jer 1:17), suggesting its created purpose is honest registration of threat checked by divine word.
Dismay appears in fallen-condition contexts—divine judgment, catastrophe, God hiding his face—with no evidence of it as an original creation design feature.
The Ezekiel 32 corpus strips imperial terror in Sheol; Jer 30:10 names salvation and quiet rest as the antidote to dismay, pointing to inner composure as eschatological goal.
No verse attributes fear as an inner experience to God; fear is consistently the creaturely response to God's reality, not a shared characteristic.
Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed), Job 23:16 (heart faint/terrified), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed by terror), Psa 6:10 (enemies suddenly dismayed) evidence sudden disruption and collapse of function.

ba.hal evidences both shock-state and rash-urgency (Ecc 5:2, 8:3); cha.tat evidences shattering (Jer 1:17); re.tet evidences panic-trembling (Jer 49:24); tim.ma.hon evidences cognitive bewilderment (Deu 28:28).
Psa 6:3 (soul dismayed), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed), Job 23:16 (heart faint) all evidence the constitutional dissolution—the inner person not holding together.

Job 4:5 (dismay overtaking Job when suffering touched him personally), Psa 2:5 (God terrifying rebels), Psa 90:7 (wrath dismaying), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed) all show dismay as a condition that happens to the person.
Dismay's structure is relatively simple: overwhelming stimulus produces inner collapse (Psa 2:5; Dan 5:6; Job 23:16), with source and recipient as structural elements rather than constituent parts.

Psa 6:3 (soul as seat), Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarming, then bodily collapse), Dan 5:10 (queen recognising dismay originates in thought), Job 23:16 (God making heart faint) locate dismay in soul and heart, engaging cognitive and volitional faculties.

Jer 1:17 (courage as opposite of dismay before opponents), Job 39:22 (war horse not dismayed—courage as complete absence of dismay), Job 32:15 (friends dismayed into speechlessness—composure as structural opposite).

Jer 1:17 (prophet commanded not to be dismayed lest God dismay him), Jer 30:10 (dismay forbidden), Isa 51:7 (not to be dismayed at revilings) explicitly exclude capitulation and paralysis before opponents.

Psa 6:3 and Dan 5:6 show dismay as dissolution of function (cannot speak, limbs fail); Job 21:6 shows dismay with shuddering but the person still reflecting—dismay involves deeper dissolution than acute fear.

2Sa 4:1 (catastrophic news collapsing confidence), Psa 2:5 (divine judicial instrument), Dan 5:6 (inner collapse visibly manifesting in body), Ecc 5:2 (ba.hal as rash agitation) show the four operational modes.
Ecc 5:2 and 7:9 show rash-agitation form; Dan 5:6 shows shattering-collapse form; Psa 2:5 shows divine-judicial form; Dan 11:44 shows alarming news converting to violent rage—contextual variation.

Job 32:15 (friends dismayed into complete speechlessness), Gen 45:3 (brothers cannot answer Joseph), Job 23:15 (overpowering upheaval in God's presence), Isa 21:3 (dismay so overwhelming cannot see) all show speech-collapse as a mode.
Gen 45:3 (brothers cannot answer—speechless), Isa 21:3 (cannot see—cognitive shutdown), Job 32:15 (friends without a word) all evidence the cognitive and verbal faculties as the first to fail.

Gen 45:3 and Job 32:15 show speechlessness; Dan 11:44 shows alarming news converting to violent external rage; Dan 5:6 shows bodily collapse—the ba.hal root spans both paralysis and violent agitation.

Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed—affective), Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarming then bodily collapse—cognitive and somatic), Job 23:16 (heart faint—affective/cognitive), Isa 21:3 (cannot see—cognitive) show affective primary, cognitive and somatic secondary.

Dan 5:6 (thoughts alarming then limbs failing and knees knocking), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarming then color changing), Dan 4:19 (dismay in thoughts then speechlessness) show inner-cognitive to somatic-expressive direction.

Dan 5:6 (triggering sight → thoughts alarmed → limbs fail, knees knock), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed → color changes), Dan 4:5/19 (dream visions → thoughts alarmed → speechlessness) demonstrate the event→inner disruption→somatic sequence.
Psa 6:3 explicitly states 'My soul also is greatly troubled'—the soul (nefesh) named as the seat of profound turmoil, driving the anguished cry to God.
Psa 6:3's soul-level location shows dismay has penetrated to the deepest experienced self, the nefesh as seat of personal life, meaning the collapse reaches the core of personal existence.

Psa 6:3 (soul/heart dismayed), Job 23:16 (God making heart faint), Dan 5:10 (queen recognising dismay originates in thought/heart), Dan 5:6 (inner person struck from heart-seat) all locate dismay in the heart.
Dan 5:10 (dismay originating in thought—knowing function disrupted) and Job 23:16 (God making heart faint—feeling/sustaining function) show knowing and feeling functions engaged together.
Dan 4:19 (thoughts racing as dismay holds Daniel), Dan 5:10 (dismay originating in thought), Dan 7:28 (thoughts greatly alarming Daniel, color changing) all locate dismay significantly in the mind.

Dan 4:19 (thoughts racing overriding speech), Dan 5:10 (thoughts as originating site of dismay), Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarming then destabilizing body) show the thought-function as the primary cognitive faculty engaged.
Psa 6:3 ('My soul also is greatly troubled') is the clearest instance of a soul-level location of dismay distinct from heart and mind.
Psa 6:3's nefesh-location reveals dismay penetrating to the personal-life center, disrupting the person's capacity to sustain their own inner aliveness at the deepest constitutional level.
Dan 5:6 (limbs giving way, knees knocking), Isa 21:3 (cannot see—eyes affected), Job 32:15 (speechlessness—speech faculties) name multiple body-parts as sites of dismay's manifestation.
Dan 5:6 (limbs failing, knees knocking), Isa 21:3 (cannot see), Job 32:15 (speechlessness) are expressive of the completeness of inner collapse and indicative of its severity.

Dan 5:6 (inner thoughts alarmed → limbs fail), Job 23:16 (God making heart faint → terror), Job 21:6 (remembering → dismay → shuddering seizing flesh) all show inner-person to body direction with possible feedback.
Dan 5:6 and 7:28 confirm dismay as inner events producing somatic consequences; the persistence pattern suggests feedback loop from body back to soul once trembling begins.
Job 4:5 (suffering touching Job personally), 2Sa 4:1 (Abner's death as news), Psa 2:5 (God's wrathful speech), Psa 90:7 (God's wrath) all show dismay as externally imposed, not internally generated.

Isa 21:3 (the vision seen produces dismay so overwhelming the prophet cannot see—sight engaged) and Dan 7:7 (the beast's appearance producing fear as immediate inner response) evidence sight and hearing engagement.

Isa 21:3 (cannot see), Job 32:15 (speechlessness—verbal-perceptive loop shut down), Dan 5:6 (color change and limb-failure as system overwhelm), Mat 1:20 (fear distorting) all show impairment of perceptive faculty.
Psa 31:11 explicitly states the psalmist has become an object of dread to his acquaintances who flee from him—dread operating as social and relational rupture.

Isa 21:3 (cannot see—cognitive-perceptive shutdown), Job 32:15 (rational and verbal faculties shut down), Dan 7:28 (dismay working through thoughts but Daniel resolving to keep it inward) all show cognitive-shutdown character of dismay.
Isa 21:3 and Job 32:15 show dismay shutting down perception and speech; Dan 7:28 and 4:19 show cognitive origin and cognitive consequence—the system shutting down.
Isa 21:3 (dismay shutting down perception, impairing capacity to retrieve memories) and Job 32:15 (dismay shutting down rational faculties) evidence cognitive shutdown impairing memory function.
Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed—profound turmoil driving anguished cry to God) and Job 21:6 (remembering producing dismay with shuddering) show the affective content of anguish, horror, and upheaval.
Dan 5:6 (dismay striking so violently that color changes and limbs fail—all other affect displaced) shows affect overwhelmed; Mat 28:8 (fear and joy coexisting) is the exception.
Isa 21:3 (cannot see—will to perceive collapsed), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—will to act disabled), Gen 45:3 (brothers cannot answer—will to speak shut down) all show volitional dissolution.
Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—volitional capacity physically disabled) and Job 32:15 (friends speechless—will to speak dissolved) are the clearest evidence of dismay dissolving volition.

Gen 35:5 (terror paralyzing the will to pursue), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed—agency physically disabled), Jer 46:5 (warriors abandoning posts and fleeing—inner resolve shattered) all evidence dismay impairing or disabling agency.

Gen 35:5 (God's terror producing immediate paralysis—bypassing deliberative agency), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed), Jer 46:5 (warriors fleeing—inner resolve shattered) show terror and dismay bypassing and impairing agency.

Psa 2:5 (God's wrathful speech terrifying rebel kings) and Jer 49:37 (God terrifying Elam before enemies—dismay as the inner condition through which he breaks their will) show God producing dismay as judicial action.

Psa 2:5 and Jer 49:37 show God deploying terror against rebels in response to defiance; Exo 23:27 and 15:16 show God sending terror against enemies opposing his covenantal purposes—conditional-responsive basis.
God's deployment of terror (Psa 2:5, Exo 23:27) and imposition of dismay as judgment (Jer 49:37) reveal his disposition as sovereignly just, using fear as an instrument of historical governance.

Psa 6:3 (soul's dismay driving anguished cry to God asking 'how long') and Psa 6:2 (dismay prompting prayer for healing) show dismay triggering movement toward God in crisis rather than sustained worship.

Jer 1:17 (prophet commanded not to let dismay grip him—transformation from collapse to sustained composure), Jer 23:4 (no longer fear or be dismayed under faithful shepherds), Jer 23:4 all evidence condition-change through commanded-against-dismay.

Job 4:5 (adversity reaching Job personally breaking his inner composure), Psa 6:3 (soul greatly dismayed—profound turmoil), Psa 6:2 (bones troubled and soul dismayed from physical suffering) show suffering as primary context and producer.

Job 4:5 (suffering producing dismay in Job), Psa 6:3 (dismay from suffering), Psa 6:10 (enemies suddenly dismayed), Job 22:10 (sudden terror from snares) show suffering producing the disordered forms of fear.

Jer 30:10 (dismay forbidden with salvation and quiet rest as antidote—no-more-dismay as eschatological state) points toward undismayed composure grounded in God's salvation as the eschatological fullness.

Jer 48:20 and 48:39 (dismay and shame paired), Jer 8:9 (dismay and shame), Jer 51:32 (panic in soldiers), Job 21:6 (dismay and shuddering) evidence co-occurrence with shame, trembling, and bodily collapse.

Dan 5:6 (overwhelming sight → thoughts alarmed → limbs fail, knees knock) and Dan 7:28 (thoughts alarmed → color changes → kept in heart) demonstrate dismay following overwhelming encounter and preceding bodily collapse.

Dan 7:28 ('my thoughts greatly alarmed me, and my color changed') explicitly shows thought-disruption as the causal intermediary between stimulus and somatic expression, confirming causal relationship.
Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure), Job 32:15 (speechlessness), Eze 7:27 (paralysis of hands) are the direct products of dismay in the verse evidence.

The root ירא generates ya.re (Gen 15:1), yir.ah (Deu 2:25), ya.re adjective (Deu 2:25/11:25); פחד generates pa.chad verb (Psa 14:5) and noun (Deu 11:25); φοβ- generates fobeō (Mat 14:27), fobos (Mat 14:26), ekfobos (Mar 9:6).
1Sa 28:5 (Saul's heart trembling—E) vs 1Sa 28:20 (Saul prostrate, strength gone—D) shows trembling expressing inner state while function remains vs dismay dissolving function entirely.

1Sa 28:5 (Saul's heart trembling—still functional) and 1Sa 28:20 (Saul collapsed, unable to eat, all strength gone) show the same person moving from trembling to collapse as dismay deepens—precise boundary evidence.

1Sa 28:5 vs 28:20 evidences degree distinction (trembling while functioning vs collapse); Job 21:6 (dismay with shuddering but still reflecting) shows kind distinction between somatic expression and inner dissolution.

Psa 6:3 (ba.hal—sudden overwhelming disruption), Dan 5:6 (be.hal—Aramaic cognate), Job 32:15 (cha.tat—rational faculties shattered), Deu 28:28 (tim.ma.hon—cognitive bewilderment) evidence the primary terms and their root meanings.

Psa 6:3 (ba.hal as intensive Piel—dismay is intensive), Jer 1:17 (cha.tat as Niphal passive—'be dismayed/be shattered'), Psa 2:5 (God terrifying—causative) show the passive-Niphal grammar confirming dismay happens to the person.
Dan 5:6 ('his thoughts alarmed him; his limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together')—be.hal of thoughts is causal, producing all somatic consequences that follow in the sentence.

Psa 6:3/10 (lament psalms—hyperbolic emotional language), Job 21:6 and 23:15-16 (wisdom dialogue—speaker identity matters), Dan 4:19 and 7:28 (Aramaic narrative—visionary-apocalyptic genre) show the three primary literary forms.

Dan 5:6 ('the king's color changed, and his thoughts alarmed him; his limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together') most fully shows the inner-to-outer cascade making dismay fully visible in its bodily consequences.

Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure, knees knocking), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed), Job 32:15 (speechlessness) map onto the dorsal vagal shutdown response (freeze/collapse) that polyvagal theory describes.

Dan 5:6 (color change, limb-failure, knees knocking), Job 32:15 (complete speechlessness), Eze 7:27 (hands paralyzed) are the somatic pattern that Porges's dorsal vagal shutdown framework makes anatomically coherent.

Dan 7:28 (Daniel keeping the matter in his heart after color changes) and Dan 5:10 (queen urging the king to master inner dismay) partially address the dissociative dimension but leave it incompletely examined.
